text: The video shows the hernia from the abdominal portion, with visible protrusion of the stomach through the diaphragm into the thoracic cavity.
The surgeon begins the dissection by mobilizing the gastropatic ligament, advancing towards the right crus, with visible separation of the ligament from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon grasps the hernia sac and begins reducing it within an avascular plane, carefully maneuvering the tissue to reposition the herniated structures.
The surgeon performs dissection using a combination of blunt dissection and a harmonic scalpel, carefully separating tissue layers in the surgical field.
The surgeon continues the dissection in the avascular plane, ensuring minimal bleeding while separating tissue layers around the hernia sac.
The surgeon continues to reduce the hernia sac into the abdominal cavity, maintaining dissection within an avascular plane.
The surgeon dissects at the base of the crura, aiming to identify the decussation, using precise techniques to navigate the anatomical structures.
The surgeon proceeds to take down the short gastric vessels using a combination of blunt dissection and harmonic scalpel, carefully separating them from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon continues to take down the short gastric vessels, progressing the dissection up to the level of the left cruce, maintaining an avascular plane.
The surgeon observes that the short gastric vessels are elongated due to the chronic and large nature of the hernia.
The surgeon prepares to pass a Penrose drain after reducing the hernia sac into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon positions a retraction device behind the esophagus to facilitate exposure and manipulation during the procedure.
The surgeon exposes both the left and right crura, cleaning up the decussation area, with visible separation and clearing of tissue around these anatomical structures.
The surgeon performs medi-central dissection of the esophagus using blunt dissection and a harmonic scalpel, carefully separating the esophagus from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues the dissection up to the level of the inferior pulmonary veins, ensuring a tension-free repair by carefully separating the surrounding tissues.
The surgeon mobilizes the esophagus intra-abdominally, advancing it by approximately three to four centimeters.
The camera captures a view of the esophagus positioned below, with surrounding anatomical structures visible in the surgical field.
The pericardium is visible at the top of the screen, providing a clear view of its anatomical position relative to the esophagus during the dissection.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the esophagus, extending it to the level of the inferior pulmonary veins, visible in the surgical field.
The left inferior pulmonary vein is visible in the surgical field as the dissection progresses.
The surgeon continues the dissection towards the right inferior pulmonary vein, which is visible in the surgical field.
The video shows a clear view of the intra-abdominal esophageal length, indicating sufficient esophagus exposure within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon performs a corral repair using O-ethabon sutures, with the pericardium visible at the top of the screen.
The surgeon places lateral sutures to re-approximate the hiatus, adjusting the anatomical position for improved closure.
The surgeon maneuvers the fundus of the stomach posteriorly around the esophagus, positioning it for the fundoplication wrap.
The surgeon performs the classic shoe shine maneuver, manipulating the fundus of the stomach around the esophagus to create the wrap characteristic of a Nissen fundoplication.
The surgeon tacks the fundus of the stomach to the diaphragm on both sides, securing the stomach in place as part of the Nissen fundoplication procedure.
The surgeon uses two O-ethabon sutures to perform the fundoplication, securing the stomach fundus around the esophagus.
The surgeon places a total of four sutures, two on each side, connecting the esophagus to the stomach as part of the fundoplication procedure.
